title: The Dress

jokes.text: Moira's elaborate shower ritual listed with clinical precision
jokes.setup: Johnny complains the water is ice cold and asks how long she's been in there
jokes.punchline: Moira rattles off a five-step ritual culminating in a 'double rinse cycle' — an absurdly elaborate regime for a motel shower
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Moira: she frames a self-indulgent spa routine with bureaucratic formality ('initial scrub,' 'deep conditioner,' 'double rinse cycle') as though it's non-negotiable procedure. The escalating specificity is the joke — each step more absurd than the last for a motel bathroom context. High character integration; only Moira speaks this way.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, there's no hot water. I just took a normal shower.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, of course there's no hot water, John. I was in there for 45 minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: 45 minutes? What were you doing in there?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: My beauty routine. The double cleanse, the toning mist, the serum, the eye cream, the moisturizer, the neck cream, the face mask, the exfoliant, the essence, the sheet mask, the overnight treatment, and then I had to condition my hair, deep condition my hair, apply my hair oil, and do my full body exfoliation and moisturizing regimen.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's... that's insane, Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, this is what happens when you let yourself go, John. One day you're neglecting your skincare routine, and the next day you're unrecognizable.

jokes.text: Yes, painfully aware of our budgetary constraints, John. I'm currently reading a magazine from July, 1987.
jokes.setup: Johnny tells Moira they can't keep running up the water bill due to budget constraints
jokes.punchline: Moira acknowledges the budget crisis, then illustrates it with the detail that she's reading a 35-year-old magazine — a deeply sad but hilariously specific proof of their reduced circumstances
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy and character integration. The joke works on two levels: Moira's hauteur ('painfully aware') is immediately undercut by a devastatingly specific and pathetic detail. The specific year (1987) elevates it from generic to vivid. It's the kind of line that becomes a character touchstone — you can picture Moira holding that worn magazine with aristocratic dignity. Earns its high score.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I've decided we need to tighten our belts. So I'm reading a magazine from 1987.

jokes.text: Where do you even find these?
jokes.setup: Moira has just revealed she's reading a 1987 magazine
jokes.punchline: Johnny's bewildered follow-up question treats the mystery of the magazine's origin as the most pressing issue, implying they're impossible to source — which makes her having one even funnier
jokes.explanation: A solid button on the previous joke. Johnny's question extends the absurdity by implying these don't exist in circulation, which retroactively makes Moira's possession of one stranger and funnier. Functional and well-placed but entirely dependent on joke 2 to work.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, a package for me? I love packages!

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, what is this? I don't remember ordering anything.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You know what, it doesn't matter. I'm just going to sign for it.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: There. Signed. Now it's officially mine, even though I have absolutely no idea what it is or if it's actually for me.

jokes.text: Alexis asks to keep the package because she signed for it, then asks what it is
jokes.setup: Alexis announces she signed for a delivery and asks if that means she gets to keep it
jokes.punchline: She immediately follows with 'Also, what is it?' — revealing she has no idea what she's claiming ownership of
jokes.explanation: Clean Alexis joke: her acquisitive instinct fires before her curiosity does. The logic ('I signed for it, so it's mine') followed immediately by ignorance of what 'it' is captures her character perfectly in two lines. Good structure and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: This is my new dress. I call it 'dobrodošli.'

jokes.text: Family, I'd like you to meet a dear friend whom I've yet to meet. Give a warm dobrodosli to my red carpet gown.
jokes.setup: The family gathers around the delivery box from Harrods
jokes.punchline: Moira introduces the dress as a person she hasn't met yet, using a foreign-language greeting ('dobrodosli' — Croatian for 'welcome') for maximum theatrical flair
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Moira: personifying the dress, using a foreign-language salutation she'd know from some obscure film role, treating an inanimate object with more warmth than her family. The phrase 'dear friend whom I've yet to meet' is a wonderfully paradoxical construction only Moira would use. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: This is my dear friend... I've never met her.

jokes.text: I've been told definitively it will happen, and I quote, 'ideally sometime this year.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is excited that the premiere has been set and asks about the date
jokes.punchline: Moira reveals the 'definitive' date is the vague qualifier 'ideally sometime this year' — which she quotes with complete sincerity as though it's a solid commitment
jokes.explanation: Sharp irony: the word 'definitively' is immediately demolished by the vaguest possible non-commitment, delivered by Moira with complete earnestness. It works as character comedy (she chooses to believe in it), industry satire (this is how Hollywood actually talks), and straight joke structure. The quotation marks make it better — she's quoting this as ironclad. Genuinely excellent line.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I have definitive information about the premiere.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It will either happen, or it won't.

jokes.text: Mommy had to shop off the rack. Fortunately, I discovered something called a 'promo code.'
jokes.setup: Alexis asks what designer gave her such a discount on the $3,700 gown
jokes.punchline: Moira reveals she bought it off the rack and used a promo code — framed as a courageous personal sacrifice and a novel discovery, not a common shopping practice
jokes.explanation: Moira's relationship to ordinary commerce is always a gold mine. 'Mommy had to shop off the rack' lands as a confession of deep personal shame, and 'I discovered something called a promo code' treats a mundane digital coupon as an archaeological find. Both beats work individually and together as an escalating portrait of her fish-out-of-water adjustment to reduced means.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so this dress is $3,700? For this price, it must be like, majorly discounted, right?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Alexis, this is off the rack.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Off the rack?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes. Which means it's a tragedy we're all going to have to learn to live with.

jokes.text: Discount couture, do you believe it? I'm surprised she made it through customs.
jokes.setup: The family reacts to the dress being purchased at a discount
jokes.punchline: Moira anthropomorphizes the dress again — this time as a traveler that might be detained at customs — suggesting the discount tarnished it enough to raise red flags
jokes.explanation: The customs joke is a fun extension of Moira's tendency to treat the dress as a person, and the idea that a discount might corrupt a garment's immigration status is pleasantly absurdist. Slightly looser than her other lines in this scene but still very Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I found this dress at an estate sale, and I got it for half price because I discovered a promo code online. She treats it like I've just split the atom.

jokes.text: I once passed off a mini horse and three Guinea pigs as service animals, so anything is possible.
jokes.setup: Moira says she's surprised the discounted dress made it through customs
jokes.punchline: Alexis casually references having committed customs fraud with a miniature horse and guinea pigs as service animals — as evidence that anything can pass through
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The specificity is perfect: not just a service animal scheme but a miniature horse AND three guinea pigs, and Alexis delivers it as throwaway evidence rather than a confession. It's dense with implication — who brings a mini horse internationally? How did this happen? — and all of that rich backstory is compressed into one offhand sentence. Earns its high scores for originality, economy, and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The dress has quite a journey ahead of it. It could be stopped by customs, questioned about its origins, its intentions...

jokes.text: I was actually just boiling water for my bath.
jokes.setup: Moira asks for a spot of tea for the dress unveiling
jokes.punchline: Johnny reveals he was boiling water not for tea but for a bath — implying the water heater situation has degraded to pioneer-era DIY bathing
jokes.explanation: A quiet, well-landed joke. The image of Johnny Rose boiling water for a bath — like a frontier settler — is quietly devastating and funny. The understated delivery makes it land better than if it were played for more. Good economy; the joke does its work in one short sentence.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, that reminds me. One time I was trying to bring like, five macaws and a llama through customs in Peru, and the guy was like 'you can't do that,' and I was like 'okay, but what if I just told him they were emotional support animals?' And apparently that's not a legal defense when you're also wearing a fur coat made of... well, we don't need to get into that.

jokes.text: TBD on the guest list, Alexis, but I love your enthusiasm.
jokes.setup: Alexis asks if she should start thinking about her look for the premiere
jokes.punchline: Moira neither confirms nor denies Alexis's invitation — 'TBD' is the noncommittal brush-off of a celebrity managing expectations, delivered warmly but clearly
jokes.explanation: A small but precise character beat: Moira's 'TBD' reveals she hasn't automatically assumed her daughter is on the premiere guest list. The collision of star-mom self-absorption and genuine warmth ('I love your enthusiasm') is quintessentially Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So what do you think of my premiere look?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's very... enthusiastic.

jokes.text: I learned the hard way from my last trip to Japan. Showed up, no cherry blossoms, turned right back around, it was such a waste.
jokes.setup: David explains he called ahead to confirm the cherry blossoms are open
jokes.punchline: He references a prior trip to Japan — a trip to an entirely different country — that he abandoned upon discovering the blossoms weren't out. The scale of the overreaction is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Terrific David joke. The casual mention of turning around from Japan as though it's the obvious rational response — 'turned right back around, it was such a waste' — is both an insight into his former wealthy lifestyle and a perfect illustration of his dramatic character. The understatement ('such a waste') lands harder than any exclamation would.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm going to see the cherry blossoms. It's a spiritual journey, really. A pilgrimage to witness nature's ephemeral beauty and reconnect with my inner self.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You're going to look at flowers.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm going to *experience* flowers, Patrick. There's a difference.

jokes.text: Wow, that's a lot of pressure to put on cherry blossoms.
jokes.setup: David explains the entire trip is structured around the cherry blossoms being in bloom
jokes.punchline: Stevie's dry, empathetic observation treats the blossoms as overwhelmed employees being given impossible expectations
jokes.explanation: A perfectly calibrated Stevie line. Her deadpan sympathy for the cherry blossoms reframes David's intensity as an unreasonable boss — and it's the kind of absurdist humanizing of nature that this show does well. Clean setup-to-punchline ratio.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I traveled to Japan to see the cherry blossoms in peak bloom. I arrived, assessed the situation, and left immediately because the timing was off.

jokes.text: Why else would we be driving to Elm Valley, Stevie? [long pause] The only other reason I can think of is that... Emir might be there.
jokes.setup: David asks rhetorically why else they'd be driving to Elm Valley, implying it's only for cherry blossoms
jokes.punchline: The pause before Stevie admits the real reason — a guy — reveals the cherry blossom trip was a decoy all along
jokes.explanation: The comedy is structural: David asks a rhetorical question meant to shut down suspicion, and the pause before Stevie answers opens the truth back up. The awkward silence is part of the joke — it signals she's about to confess. Works nicely as a scene-turning moment.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So you're telling me you've been stress-eating macarons for three days because you're worried the cherry blossoms won't be 'pink enough'?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: They need to be a very specific shade of blush. It's not just pink, Stevie.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Right. Because nature is definitely going to consult your mood board.

jokes.text: You had the dirty motel sex with?
jokes.setup: Stevie describes Emir as 'the hotel reviewer that...' and trails off
jokes.punchline: David helpfully and bluntly completes her sentence with 'dirty motel sex'
jokes.explanation: David's willingness to bluntly name what Stevie is euphemistically circling is a great character beat and a clean punchline. The specificity of 'dirty motel sex' (rather than just 'had sex with') adds color. His completion of her sentence is both helpful and cutting.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why would we go to Elm Valley?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Because you said you wanted to go to Elm Valley.

jokes.text: Well, when you put it that way it sorta cheapens it.
jokes.setup: David bluntly says 'dirty motel sex'
jokes.punchline: Stevie's mock-wounded response implies there was something romantic about dirty motel sex that David has now ruined
jokes.explanation: A solid button on David's bluntness. Stevie's deadpan implying she had fond feelings for the 'dirty motel sex' framing is a small, well-earned character beat. The joke works because of her completely dry delivery.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: She was accosted by a man with very aggressive cologne and a Members Only jacket.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I wouldn't say accosted.

jokes.text: He might also be there, reviewing a hotel. But not the hotel that we're going to, because that would be a crazy coincidence.
jokes.setup: Stevie admits Emir might be in Elm Valley
jokes.punchline: She immediately adds 'not the hotel we're going to' — a transparently false reassurance that confirms exactly the opposite
jokes.explanation: The joke lands on Stevie's obvious bad faith — calling it 'a crazy coincidence' signals she knows it's not a coincidence at all. A reliable sitcom beat, well-executed but not especially original.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not going to the gardens alone. What am I, a pervert?

jokes.text: Wow! So this is an abduction, then. I am a single woman driving way out of town to meet a guy I don't know very well!
jokes.setup: David realizes they're not going to the botanical gardens — this was all a ruse to get him to come along on Stevie's date
jokes.punchline: David reframes the situation as an abduction and then immediately adopts Stevie's perspective ('I am a single woman...') — melodramatically voicing her vulnerability as though narrating a true crime podcast
jokes.explanation: David's dramatic self-insert into Stevie's romantic situation — performing her fear more than she does — is perfectly in character. 'This is an abduction' is the best word for his melodrama, and his ventriloquizing of her single-woman concerns while being a gay man is a rich comic irony. High character integration and a quotable line.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm calling the police. This is kidnapping. You're going to prison, Stevie. I hope you like orange, because that's going to be your new signature color, and frankly, it does nothing for your complexion.

jokes.text: What, am I gonna walk around and admire the cherry blossoms alone, like some pervert?!
jokes.setup: Stevie suggests David can still go to the botanical gardens once Emir shows up
jokes.punchline: David equates walking alone through a botanical garden with being a pervert — an absurd moral equivalence that reveals his deep terror of being seen doing anything uncool by himself
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke. The leap from 'walking alone through a garden' to 'like some pervert' is perfectly absurd and perfectly David. It crystallizes his character — his pathological need for social validation, his fear of seeming uncool or pitiful — in one wildly funny image. The word 'pervert' is the inspired choice: it's the LAST word you'd associate with botanical gardens, which makes it so surprising and so right. Easily quotable, highly rewatchable, and earns its high scores.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Is this decaf?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, yes. Totally decaf.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Actually, no. I'm sorry, I completely forgot.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Can I just have that back?

jokes.text: You know what, I hope for your sake, that they have cherry blossoms in prison.
jokes.setup: David has established this trip is an 'abduction'
jokes.punchline: He threatens Stevie with prison — implying he will press charges for this 'abduction' — and his only expressed concern is whether she'll have access to cherry blossoms there
jokes.explanation: A beautiful callback that pays off both the 'abduction' bit and the cherry blossom setup. The joke implies David is so committed to the botanical garden plan that even his prison threat is filtered through it. It's dark, specific, and structurally tight — the cherry blossom detail does the heavy lifting. One of the episode's top lines.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Shannon's been looking so old lately.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, she's the same age as me, so...

jokes.text: Oh, almost a sweet gesture, Alexis, thank you.
jokes.setup: Alexis takes back the non-decaf coffee
jokes.punchline: Ted thanks her anyway for the 'almost' sweet gesture — the qualifier doing all the comedic work
jokes.explanation: The word 'almost' is doing heavy lifting here. Ted's gracious acknowledgment of a failed gesture, with the one-word qualifier inserted, is clean and dry. Functions as a button on the coffee bit.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: She just has this, like, really confident assertion that disliking her is always the other person's fault.

jokes.text: You know who's not sweet? Old Shan out there.
jokes.setup: Ted thanks Alexis for the 'almost sweet gesture'
jokes.punchline: Alexis pivots immediately to complaining about Shannon — calling her 'Old Shan' — establishing both her jealousy and her tendency to immediately redirect to grievances
jokes.explanation: Alexis's 'Old Shan' is a nicely catty nickname deployed instantly. The rapid pivot from receiving thanks to trash-talking someone is very Alexis. Functional character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, you did it! You got the girl! Look at you, little Ted, all grown up and making moves.

jokes.text: I'm just gonna say it, Ted. Most people are a fan. So when someone's not, it's usually on them.
jokes.setup: Alexis is pressing the case that Shannon simply doesn't like her
jokes.punchline: Alexis's solution is to declare, with complete earnestness, that disliking Alexis Rose is statistically aberrant and therefore a personal failing of the non-fan
jokes.explanation: A beautifully calibrated Alexis line. The word 'fan' is doing a lot of work — she literally describes human relationships in terms of fandom, which perfectly captures her residual celebrity self-conception. Delivered without a trace of irony, it's funnier than if she knew she was being absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so you're telling me you've never actually been in a real relationship? Like, ever?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, there was that time in high school—

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Okay, I'm literally going to need to create a medical file for you. 'Theodore... P... Rose.' No, wait—'Theodore... M... Rose.' You need a middle initial that sounds serious. Something that screams 'emotional damage.'

jokes.text: Yes you did, you little smooch monster!
jokes.setup: Ted admits he and Shannon 'hooked up'
jokes.punchline: Alexis's delighted response — 'you little smooch monster!' — is so incongruously cheerful and babyish that it undercuts any expected jealousy and reveals her complicated emotional register
jokes.explanation: 'Smooch monster' is an inspired word choice — childish, affectionate, and completely at odds with the situation. Alexis's unexpected delight rather than jealousy is a character-specific subversion that makes the scene funnier and more interesting. Quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I'm actually a doctor.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I'm a doctor too.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: No, you're not.

jokes.text: Well, we did a lot more than that. / I'm sorry, should I be jealous?
jokes.setup: Alexis characterizes their encounter as a 'smooch'
jokes.punchline: Ted corrects her to something more than smooching; Alexis's response 'should I be jealous?' is delivered as a genuine question, revealing she hasn't yet decided what to feel
jokes.explanation: The joke is in Alexis's genuine uncertainty — she's not performing jealousy, she's actually doing the math in real time. Her 'should I be jealous?' reads as a sincere question, which is funnier than if she knew how to feel. Ted's embarrassed clarification ('We did a lot more than that') is also a solid comedic beat.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I got some good news for you. I found an excuse to spend money.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's not good news, Roland.

jokes.text: Beep, beep! Paging Dr. Casanova J. Heartbreaker.
jokes.setup: Ted explains he ended things with Shannon because they were better off as friends
jokes.punchline: Alexis mocks Ted's self-image as a heartbreaker — using the intercom PA device framing to treat it like a hospital announcement — the 'J.' middle initial adding absurdist flair
jokes.explanation: The paging system conceit is unexpected and fun. 'Casanova J. Heartbreaker' as a formal professional name is a great construction, and the 'J.' middle initial adds a layer of mock-officialdom. Very Alexis in its playfulness. The 'beep beep' opener is the perfect touch.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: His mock-Spanish nickname 'El Tightwad'

jokes.text: Yeah, it's funny because I actually am a doctor. / So am I... / No, you're not. Just-just Ted.
jokes.setup: Alexis sarcastically calls Ted 'Doctor' as she leaves
jokes.punchline: Ted points out he literally is a doctor; Alexis claims she also is one; Ted shoots that down — and the reveal that Alexis's 'doctorate' is not real is the final beat
jokes.explanation: A quick three-beat exchange that works because each beat does something: Ted reasserts his legitimacy, Alexis tries to claim equivalent status, Ted debunks it with 'just-just Ted' suggesting this is not a new conversation. The stammered 'just-just' is a nice touch suggesting exasperated familiarity.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: This humidity is absolutely suffocating. I feel like I'm being vaginally consumed by the atmosphere.

jokes.text: Well, I might have to take out a mortgage on the dress my wife just bought.
jokes.setup: Roland says he has good news for Johnny
jokes.punchline: Johnny's self-deprecating disclosure that he may need to mortgage a garment — not a house, a dress — is quietly devastating and precise
jokes.explanation: A callback to the dress purchase that lands because the word 'mortgage' applied to a piece of clothing is a perfectly absurd unit mismatch. Johnny's defeated tone makes it funnier — he's not performing misery, he's genuinely drowning.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: That's not honeysuckle, that's Johnny's aftershave.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh, I'm sorry.

jokes.text: Oh, come on, this coming from the guy who was just bragging about taking out a mortgage to pay for his wife's dress!
jokes.setup: Johnny says he can't afford to throw away money
jokes.punchline: Roland calls out the contradiction between Johnny's frugality posturing and his casual mention of mortgaging a dress — and mischaracterizes it as 'bragging'
jokes.explanation: Roland's 'bragging' misread is the key: Johnny was lamenting, not bragging, and Roland has heard it as flex. This reveals Roland's particular interpretive filter (he respects financial swagger) while also calling back the dress joke. Double-duty comedic move.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: When was Emir supposed to arrive?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: He was supposed to be here three hours ago.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Three hours? Where is he?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I don't know. He's not answering his phone.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, this is awkward.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I'm used to people not showing up for me.

jokes.text: Moira wafting in smelling of honeysuckle while Johnny and Roland discuss the water heater crisis
jokes.setup: Johnny is stressed about expenses and repairs; Roland is haggling
jokes.punchline: Moira enters the scene as though arriving at a garden party, commenting on the honeysuckle scent — completely oblivious to the financial chaos around her
jokes.explanation: Moira's entrance is a tonal contrast joke — the scene is all financial anxiety, and she arrives like a breath of blissful obliviousness. The honeysuckle comment (turned out to be Johnny's aftershave) is a nice secondary beat. Effective character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, I guess I deserved that. I'm not exactly a catch. I'm difficult, I'm weird, I have the personality of a wet blanket, and I smell like old pennies.

jokes.text: Yeah, it might be Johnny's aftershave. I'm sorry, it is pungent.
jokes.setup: Moira suggests there's a scent of honeysuckle in the air
jokes.punchline: Roland casually insults Johnny's aftershave and then, apparently not wanting to let it go, adds the unnecessary clarifier 'it is pungent'
jokes.explanation: The 'I'm sorry, it is pungent' addition is what makes this work — the apology followed immediately by doubling down is Roland at his best. He's not mean-spirited, just constitutionally unable to self-edit. Functional.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'll get the first round of drinks.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You'll be buying all of them. As penance.

jokes.text: John, give this man a raise!
jokes.setup: Roland suggests Johnny re-examine his 'extravagant lifestyle' and mentions the 8-cylinder car
jokes.punchline: Moira — who has just bought a $3,700 gown on a family budget — tells Johnny to reward the man pressuring them to spend more money on the water heater
jokes.explanation: Moira's reflexive generosity with money she doesn't have — in a context where they're literally arguing about costs — is a neat character contradiction. The joke works because she sides with Roland against Johnny on fiscal grounds while being the biggest financial liability in the scene.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So Shannon, I've been thinking a lot about female empowerment lately.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Like, we need to support each other as women, you know?

dialogue.speaker: Shannon
dialogue.line: That's... really great, Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, so I was thinking we could like, burn our bras together or something?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, not literally burn them. These are all designer.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Maybe we could just like, donate them to a cause? Or take a photo pretending we're burning them?

jokes.text: And when dear, did our room become so moist? I'm off to get a dehumidifier.
jokes.setup: Moira is breezing through the conversation
jokes.punchline: She notices the room dampness — a symptom of the plumbing crisis they've been discussing — and treats it as a minor inconvenience to be solved by purchasing yet another appliance
jokes.explanation: The word 'moist' applied to a motel room is delightfully unpleasant, and Moira's breezy solution (buying something else) amplifies the joke. It also foreshadows the dehumidifier scene that will follow.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So Ted told me about you two, and... *makes exaggerated kissing sounds* ...that's what he said it was like.

jokes.text: He's not coming. I'm an idiot.
jokes.setup: Emir is two hours late and hasn't shown up at the hotel
jokes.punchline: Stevie's completely flat, unsentimental self-assessment — no drama, just 'he's not coming, I'm an idiot' — is comedic in its brevity and honesty
jokes.explanation: Stevie's flat, rapid self-condemnation is the joke. Most characters would get three lines to work up to this conclusion; she arrives in four words. The economy is outstanding and completely in character — Stevie has zero patience for her own self-pity. The straightforwardness is funnier than any elaborate reaction.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So, uh, Ted and Shannon just... you know, got together last night.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, he really... put his deposit in her account, if you know what I mean.

jokes.text: Well, I guess the good thing to come out of this abduction is that if anyone knows how to crawl out from under an embarrassing romantic failure, it's me.
jokes.setup: Stevie has concluded she's been stood up
jokes.punchline: David reframes his earlier 'abduction' complaint as now having an upside — and the joke is him both calling back his own dramatic framing AND positioning his romantic disaster history as a useful credential
jokes.explanation: A warm, character-rich moment that still functions as comedy. David's ironic self-awareness — presenting his extensive catalog of romantic humiliations as expertise — is both funny and touching. The callback to 'abduction' earns extra points for tying the thread together.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Alexis, I think we should keep our conversations workplace appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, Ted, it was just a comment about my weekend.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Yes, well, the details about your hot stone massage and the 'very flexible' massage therapist are probably better suited for lunch conversation.

jokes.text: The hotel bar has karaoke. I say we get several rounds of polar bear shots, load up some Mariah, and I will spend the rest of the night pretending not to resent you.
jokes.setup: David proposes a plan to salvage the evening
jokes.punchline: Stevie's counter-proposal includes the genuinely thoughtful gesture of karaoke, then undercuts it with 'pretending not to resent you' — a perfectly calibrated expression of her actual emotional state
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The specific details (polar bear shots, Mariah) are already funny, but 'I will spend the rest of the night pretending not to resent you' is exceptional — it's simultaneously generous and hostile, warm and funny. The 'pretending' qualifier does everything: she's not going to actually resent him, but she's also not going to pretend she isn't a little. Perfectly Stevie, perfectly calibrated.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, he was really going at it, you know? Like, just completely—

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Alexis, I think we should probably keep this conversation out of the office.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, Ted, it's not that bad.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: It's just... there are professional boundaries we need to maintain here.

jokes.text: Oh, you'll be getting every round.
jokes.setup: David says he'll get the first round
jokes.punchline: Stevie corrects him — not just this round but every round, as debt repayment for the scheme
jokes.explanation: A lean, punchy button on Stevie's previous line. The correction from 'first round' to 'every round' is economical, fair, and funny. A small but satisfying beat.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: LOL

jokes.text: Can I get a 'hell yeah' for two hard-working women in the 21st Century?
jokes.setup: Alexis approaches Shannon to clear the air, opening with a performance of solidarity
jokes.punchline: Alexis deploys a girl-power rallying cry as social lubrication — the inauthenticity of the gesture is the joke, especially given her actual motive (to defuse a romantic rival situation)
jokes.explanation: Alexis using feminist rhetoric as a social tool rather than a genuine value is a solid character-specific irony. The '21st Century' qualifier positions it as generically inspirational, which makes it funnier. Functional.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm watching a beginner's woodworking video and the man is moving far too quickly. I cannot keep up.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: He's already moved on to the next step and I'm still trying to understand what a 'miter' is.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: This is a beginner's video, David. If I cannot understand a beginner's video, what does that make me?

jokes.text: Ted told me all about your little... Mmm! Muah! Muah! And it's like... not a big deal.
jokes.setup: Alexis is trying to address the Shannon/Ted hookup diplomatically
jokes.punchline: Instead of saying the word 'kissing' or 'hookup,' she mimes it with lip-smacking 'Mmm Muah Muah' sounds — an absurdly childish pantomime for someone trying to appear mature and unbothered
jokes.explanation: The physical comedy of Alexis pantomiming the hookup with exaggerated kissing sounds — while trying to be sophisticated and nonchalant — is a great character-specific gag. Only Alexis would handle this with both faux-maturity and total childishness in the same sentence. The sounds themselves are funny, and the 'not a big deal' after them is funnier for the gap.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: That's quite the vehicle you got there, Johnny. Must've cost you a pretty penny.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, it's a classic. They don't make them like this anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh, so you're bragging about how old it is now? That's what we're doing?

jokes.text: Did you tell him that after he told you that? [Shannon reveals she also told Ted they should be 'just friends']
jokes.setup: Shannon says she told Ted they'd be better off as friends
jokes.punchline: Alexis realizes that Ted told her the SAME thing — both of them said it, meaning neither of them actually ended it and both thought they were rejected
jokes.explanation: A well-structured comedic reveal: the mutual 'just friends' excuse means neither person actually ended it decisively — they were talking past each other. Alexis's one question ('Did you tell him that after he told you that?') unpacks an entire comic misunderstanding. The comedic timing of this as a realization rather than an accusation is sharp.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, that was... surprisingly competent for someone who appears to be actively dying.

jokes.text: Look, I don't know about you, Shannon, but I am so glad we did this. Had I not talked to her, I never would've found out that she called it off.
jokes.setup: Ted is trying to gain control of the conversation
jokes.punchline: Alexis hijacks the scene by declaring victory — specifically the 'discovery' that Shannon supposedly called it off — which is both a manipulation of the facts and a delightful dodge
jokes.explanation: Alexis's spin on the 'who ended it' ambiguity — she simply declares Shannon called it off, glossing over the mutual confusion — is a great character move. She's not lying exactly, but she's certainly not being fair. The speed with which she closes the conversation is funny.

dialogue.speaker: Emir
dialogue.line: I couldn't call you because after the accident, we somehow ended up with each other's phones.

dialogue.speaker: Emir
dialogue.line: I've been trying to figure out why my phone kept getting calls from people asking for someone named David all day.

jokes.text: We were supposed to meet up at the 5k for Feral Cats, and... I never followed up.
jokes.setup: Ted tries to explain the vague aftermath of his night with Shannon
jokes.punchline: The specificity of the charity event — '5k for Feral Cats' — is the joke. It's such a particular, small-town-vet kind of event that it's funnier than any generic 'we met up' would be.
jokes.explanation: '5k for Feral Cats' is a perfect Schitt's Creek joke — hyper-specific, small-town, vet-coded, and totally plausible for Ted. The comedy is in the specificity: this isn't 'a charity run,' it's a run for feral cats. The detail implies a whole world.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm... uh... David... Rosé. No, wait. Rosé David. I mean— my name is David Rosé-David. It's a hyphenated thing.

jokes.text: And then we saw each other the next weekend at the opening of Bronwyn's Exotic Animal Clinic, and I assumed you'd gotten the hint.
jokes.setup: Shannon explains why she thought Ted had communicated his disinterest
jokes.punchline: The 'Bronwyn's Exotic Animal Clinic' opening as a social venue — a named location that implies regular attendance at niche veterinary events as a dating context — is deeply funny in its specificity
jokes.explanation: Another world-specific detail joke: veterinary social events as the dating pool. 'Bronwyn's Exotic Animal Clinic' opening is the kind of specific invented detail that makes sitcom worlds feel lived-in. It pairs nicely with the Feral Cats 5k for a running gag of vet-world absurdism.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, can we talk for a second?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: If this is about the dehumidifier, I have already lodged a formal complaint with the manufacturer, the Better Business Bureau, and God.

jokes.text: Okay, not to interject, but I assumed that this would play out in a much more interesting way. So for the sake of time, I'm just gonna go with Shannon's version of things.
jokes.setup: The Ted/Shannon who-ended-it conversation is devolving into an inconclusive muddle
jokes.punchline: Alexis breaks the fourth wall of the scene — not literally but practically — by acknowledging the conversation isn't delivering the drama she anticipated, and executive-producing it to a conclusion that suits her
jokes.explanation: A genuinely excellent joke. Alexis's 'I assumed this would play out in a much more interesting way' acknowledges she has a narrative expectation of life — a very specific kind of self-absorbed dramatic intelligence — and then her 'for the sake of time' is a perfect executive move. She's essentially editing the scene in real time. High character integration, strong originality, very quotable. Earns its 8+ score.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, we need to talk about our finances. We're hemorrhaging money.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I found this divine Valentino at an estate sale. Only twelve thousand dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Twelve thousand— Moira, did you hear what I just said? We're going broke.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, darling, which is exactly why I need something to lift my spirits. It's a investment in my mental health.

jokes.text: Shh! Shh! Shh! Shh! Okay.
jokes.setup: Ted tries to re-open the conversation after Alexis has declared it closed
jokes.punchline: Alexis's four-beat 'Shh!' to silence a grown man in a professional setting — her longtime boyfriend — is both comically imperious and weirdly effective
jokes.explanation: The physical specificity of four 'Shh!' sounds in a row, followed by a calm 'Okay,' is a great actress/character beat. Alexis hushing Ted with the authority of a reality TV host cutting to commercial is entirely in character. Maximum economy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, if Alexis is expanding her home office, she needs to be aware that ink cartridges are surprisingly expensive. I once spent $847 on printer ink in a single month.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, that's insane. I don't even print anything.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that was before I realized I could just not print things. Game changer.

jokes.text: Johnny watches a DIY water heater tutorial video while kicking the heater in frustration
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to repair the water heater himself to save money
jokes.punchline: He's watching a beginner tutorial but can't keep up, rewinds, and is physically kicking the heater — the visual of a flustered man watching YouTube while assaulting an appliance
jokes.explanation: The physical comedy of Johnny Rose — a former luxury hotelier — trying to follow a DIY plumbing video is rich situational comedy. The kicking and the frantic rewinding paint a vivid image. More visual/physical than verbal, which is a nice gear-change for this episode.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, Johnny, this dress is an investment in the family's morale. When I look this fabulous, everyone's mood elevates by at least forty percent. I'm essentially a mood ring, except I'm a dress, and I cost significantly more.

jokes.text: What's our policy on smoke breaks? / Who's taking a smoke break? / I am. / You don't smoke. / Yeah, I know. And I don't think I should be penalized for that.
jokes.setup: Roland interrupts Johnny's water heater research with an 'urgent question' about smoke breaks
jokes.punchline: Roland argues he deserves smoke break time even though he doesn't smoke — treating the break as a right independent of the activity, and his indignation at being 'penalized' for non-smoking is the absurd escalation
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Roland's labor-rights framing of a non-smoking smoke break — treating the break's function as irrelevant to its entitlement — is both absurd and ironically coherent. 'I don't think I should be penalized for that' is the payoff: he positions healthy choices as a workplace disadvantage. Perfectly Roland, highly original, extremely quotable. Earns its high score.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: This isn't really relevant, but I did get an email from someone named David...

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, that's my brother.

jokes.text: Roland watches Johnny kicking the water heater: 'LOL.' (said aloud)
jokes.setup: Roland observes Johnny physically attacking the water heater in frustration
jokes.punchline: Roland says 'LOL' out loud — verbally articulating an internet abbreviation, suggesting he either doesn't know how 'lol' works or is doing it ironically
jokes.explanation: Roland saying 'LOL' aloud as a sincere expression is a sharp piece of character-specific absurdism. It could be ignorance of internet culture or aggressive deployment of it — the ambiguity makes it funnier. Economy is perfect: one word, maximum strangeness.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I wanted to reach out and say that I fal-awful about how things ended between us.

jokes.text: Yeah well, I think you got the right video there. But don't you think something like that should be handled by professionals, Johnny? Like, maybe... Terry?
jokes.setup: Johnny is watching a 'beginner' water heater tutorial
jokes.punchline: Roland validates the video choice ('you got the right video there') before pivoting to his real agenda — selling Johnny on Terry — with the pause before 'Terry?' making it clear he's been waiting to say this the whole time
jokes.explanation: Roland's mercenary pivot back to Terry is predictable but delivered well. The pause before 'Terry?' signals his strategy, which is funny in its transparency. He's not subtle, and the show knows it.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, I tried the falafel at that new place, and it was absolutely fal-awful.

jokes.text: Well, not all of us can drive around in an 8-cylinder car. / It's a '78 Lincoln, and it's all we could afford!
jokes.setup: Roland tells Johnny to re-examine his 'extravagant lifestyle'
jokes.punchline: He cites the 8-cylinder car as evidence of extravagance; Johnny defends it as the most affordable thing they could find — a 1978 vehicle as a symbol of poverty, not luxury
jokes.explanation: Roland's warped perception of wealth — seeing a 46-year-old Lincoln as luxury transport — is a nice character-based irony. Johnny's exasperated defense ('it's all we could afford!') inverts the expected dynamic: the Rose family, once genuinely wealthy, is now being lectured on extravagance by Roland Schitt. Good character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: She just texted me back 'ok cool.' That's it. 'Ok cool.'

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wow. That's... really casual.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I know! And then she asked me to notarize some documents for her business.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, after she dumped you?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: And you did it?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, yes.

jokes.text: Karaoke bar scene: Stevie singing on stage, David watching approvingly — 'Wow, for someone who can barely stand, she sounds good.'
jokes.setup: David watches Stevie perform karaoke after several rounds of polar bear shots
jokes.punchline: David's comment acknowledges Stevie can barely physically function while simultaneously praising her vocal performance — the incongruity is the joke
jokes.explanation: A well-observed line that paints the visual scene with dialogue: Stevie is listing drunkenly but still hitting the notes. The juxtaposition of her compromised physical state and her vocal competence is a clean comedic image. Also, 'for someone who can barely stand' is a great qualifier.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I just... I think you're really special, Ted. You make me feel things I've never felt before. You're kind and thoughtful and you actually care about me. I don't think I've ever felt this way about anyone, and I just wanted you to know that you mean so much to me, and—

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Also, you have a really great ass.

jokes.text: Good. I'm gonna see if they have more Mariah, the first three songs were just a warm-up.
jokes.setup: David checks in on how Stevie is feeling; she says 'better'
jokes.punchline: David reveals the first three Mariah Carey songs were a warm-up — implying he intends to sing many more — and frames this as a normal, proportionate response to the evening
jokes.explanation: The escalation of David's karaoke commitment — three songs is just the warm-up — is deeply in character. David Rose having a Mariah Carey karaoke warm-up routine is perfect. The callback to the earlier plan pays off well.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I want Ted. I want to be with him. I want to wake up next to him every morning for the rest of my life.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Also, his calves are insane.

jokes.text: The student driver has my phone, and I have his. We didn't give them back to each other after we exchanged info. So if you wanna know what's on a 16-year-old's smartphone, I could offer you that.
jokes.setup: Emir explains why he couldn't call to say he'd be late
jokes.punchline: He and the student driver accidentally kept each other's phones, and Emir offers Stevie access to a teenager's phone — as a consolation prize for being stood up
jokes.explanation: The offer of the teenager's phone as an alternative to an apology is an unexpected comedic beat. Emir's matter-of-fact 'I could offer you that' treats this as a genuine if unusual consolation, which is what makes it funny. The setup (accident) is functional but the payoff lands.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm staying. I've spent the last twenty minutes building a rapport with this crowd, and I'm not going to throw that away just because you want to leave.

jokes.text: No, thank you. / Yeah, that's probably for the best.
jokes.setup: Emir offers Stevie access to the teenager's phone
jokes.punchline: Stevie declines; Emir immediately agrees it was probably for the best — suggesting the phone's contents are genuinely inadvisable
jokes.explanation: Emir's quick agreement implies the teenager's phone contains something deeply unpleasant, which turns his earlier offer into retroactive dark comedy. The two-line exchange is perfectly economic.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, well in that case, could you also cover my minibar, my spa services, my dry cleaning, and any incidental charges?

dialogue.speaker: Emir
dialogue.line: No, no. Just the room.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Just the room.

jokes.text: Okay, good news, they have 'Fantasy,' which means that you get to play Ol' Dirty Bastard.
jokes.setup: David has been checking the karaoke catalog while Stevie meets Emir
jokes.punchline: David announces the Mariah Carey song 'Fantasy' is available — specifically framing it as an opportunity for Stevie to perform the ODB rap verse — implying he's already assigned the parts
jokes.explanation: David has already cast the karaoke performance in his head — Stevie as Ol' Dirty Bastard — which is both absurdly specific and deeply funny given the context. The matter-of-fact delivery of 'you get to play Ol' Dirty Bastard' as though it's a gift is perfect. A great David moment.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So everything is covered?

dialogue.speaker: Emir
dialogue.line: The mini-bar, yes.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, I thought you meant—

dialogue.speaker: Emir
dialogue.line: Just the mini-bar. Not the full room service.

jokes.text: Oh, are you here with someone? / Uh, no. / Oh! Ohhh... Oh um, no. We just ran into each other at the bar. Um, long story short, I took a pottery class with this one.
jokes.setup: Emir arrives and sees David with Stevie
jokes.punchline: Stevie's nervous cover story — 'we just ran into each other,' then 'long story short, I took a pottery class with this one' — is transparently improvised and specifically odd ('pottery class')
jokes.explanation: The pottery class cover story is the joke — a random, specific lie that implies Stevie's improv instinct went to an oddly mundane place. 'Long story short' followed by something that raises more questions than it answers is a good joke construction.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, that's going to be a problem because I may have already consumed the entire mini-bar.

jokes.text: Oh, you've blown it. But I'll still take a free dinner.
jokes.setup: Emir asks if he's blown his chance at dinner after being two hours late
jokes.punchline: Stevie confirms he's blown it — no softening — then immediately accepts the dinner anyway, as long as it's free
jokes.explanation: A perfectly Stevie line. The two-part structure — devastating honesty ('you've blown it') immediately followed by pragmatic acceptance ('but I'll take a free dinner') — is very funny and very true to her character. She refuses to perform romantic graciousness but she's not going to turn down a meal either. One of the most quotable lines in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Do you have any idea what time it is? I've been listening to you people stomp around like a herd of wildebeest for the past three hours. I have a headache, I'm exhausted, and I've had exactly four glasses of wine.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Four.

jokes.text: Keep the mics hot, I'm comin' in!
jokes.setup: David is alone at the karaoke bar after Stevie leaves with Emir
jokes.punchline: Rather than being dejected about being left alone — his earlier fear — David announces his karaoke return like a headliner taking the stage
jokes.explanation: A beautiful character payoff: David, who feared being seen alone 'like a pervert,' has found his community at the karaoke bar and is now announcing his return with rock-star energy. The callback makes it richer — he solved his own problem in the most David way possible. 'Keep the mics hot' is a great line.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'd like to return this dress. And I'm going to need both a mental and a physical documentation of this moment, because it may never happen again.

jokes.text: Moira runs the dehumidifier so loudly that Johnny can't be heard trying to talk to her
jokes.setup: Johnny needs to tell Moira about the additional plumbing costs
jokes.punchline: Moira bought a dehumidifier to deal with the moisture problem — itself caused by the plumbing crisis — and it's now so loud Johnny literally cannot communicate the news of the plumbing crisis to her
jokes.explanation: A beautifully circular joke: the dehumidifier Moira bought to solve the damp problem is now blocking the conversation about the larger problem that caused the damp. The Rube Goldberg quality — each solution creating a new obstacle — is great plotting-as-comedy. The callback to joke 39 makes it a satisfying payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, why are you wearing those shoes?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Because I like them.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: But they're so...

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Ugly? Yes. That's the point.

jokes.text: So, it looks like in addition to the water heater, we're gonna have to replace some of the plumbing. And the water is so hard that Terry said that it's likely in the next little while we might also need a new... water softener.
jokes.setup: Johnny finally gets to tell Moira the bad news
jokes.punchline: The bad news escalates: water heater → plumbing → water softener. Each pause before the next item suggests Johnny is steeling himself. The accumulation of costs is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The escalating repair list is structurally familiar (things keep getting worse) but well-delivered. The pause before 'water softener' — Johnny's reluctance to say it — gives the final item more comedic weight than it deserves on paper. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: On the upside, this gown has exceeded all expectations.
jokes.setup: Johnny has just delivered a cascade of expensive bad news about their plumbing
jokes.punchline: Moira's silver lining — the dress — is not only unrelated to the problem but is itself a financial problem. Her comfort item is her contribution to the crisis.
jokes.explanation: Moira's pivot to the dress as consolation is perfectly in character — she's offering comfort, but her comfort is the problem. The joke works because the audience holds both facts simultaneously. Good character integration.

jokes.text: I told Alexis now is not the time to expand that home office. Not with the price of ink today.
jokes.setup: Johnny suggests any money coming in should go to the family, implying they need to look at recent purchases
jokes.punchline: Moira preemptively offers up Alexis's (fictional) home office expansion as her contribution to the budget conversation — deflecting from her own dress purchase with a non-existent expense
jokes.explanation: Moira inventing an Alexis expense to offer up as the family's sacrifice — while protecting her own dress — is pure Moira: loving, self-serving, and completely unselfaware. 'Not with the price of ink today' is a perfect non-sequitur detail. The joke also works as a deflection we can see through immediately.

jokes.text: I've had a lot of fun hanging out with you, and I really... fal-awful saying this over text.
jokes.setup: Ted reads his breakup text aloud to Alexis
jokes.punchline: He wrote 'feel awful' but accidentally typed 'fal-awful' — which is a terrible falafel-related accidental pun, in a message to the girl from the falafel place
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest constructed jokes. The accidental pun — 'fal-awful' instead of 'feel awful,' in a message to the girl from the falafel place — is a brilliant multi-layered construction. It works as wordplay, as character comedy (Ted would absolutely do this), and as a callback to 'Melanie from the falafel place.' The joke is earned completely: it's been set up two beats earlier, and the payoff is both surprising and inevitable. Earned vs. cheap score is very high because this required genuine craft.

jokes.text: Pretty harsh. But it had to be done.
jokes.setup: Ted reads the 'fal-awful' breakup text aloud
jokes.punchline: Rather than being embarrassed by the accidental pun and generally cringe breakup text, he evaluates it as a tough but necessary communication — completely unaware of how it sounds
jokes.explanation: Ted's self-assessment of his own cringeworthy message as 'pretty harsh but necessary' — with no self-awareness about its problems — is a great button on the fal-awful joke. His sincerity makes it funnier.

jokes.text: 'Cool!' Exclamation mark. / And it looks like you ended up walking her dog for her? / Well, she seemed really upset.
jokes.setup: Ted has been framing himself as a heartbreaker who had to let Melanie down
jokes.punchline: Melanie's response was 'Cool!' — total indifference — and then Ted walked her dog anyway because he felt guilty
jokes.explanation: A perfectly constructed two-part joke. 'Cool! Exclamation mark' demolishes Ted's heartbreaker self-image in three words — Melanie clearly wasn't heartbroken. Then the reveal that he walked her dog 'because she seemed really upset' is Ted at his most specifically lovable-ridiculous: he decided she was upset despite all evidence, and performed a gesture of penance. The escalation from devastating response to absurd consolation is excellent.

jokes.text: I've gone through enough heartbreakers for like, two people's lifetimes. And I don't want that anymore. I want you. And that cute lil' butt.
jokes.setup: Alexis is delivering what sounds like a sincere romantic declaration to Ted
jokes.punchline: She ends a heartfelt moment with 'and that cute lil' butt' — an utterly Alexis addition that undercuts the sentiment without destroying it
jokes.explanation: Alexis's romantic declarations always bottom out somewhere unexpected, and 'that cute lil' butt' is a perfectly calibrated landing spot — affectionate, slightly shallow, and entirely real for her. The diminutive 'lil'' is the right touch. A warm joke that lands without feeling cheap.

jokes.text: Stevie performing karaoke; David shouts 'Yes, Tammy! Tammy, everybody! It's Tammy! Yeah!'
jokes.setup: Stevie is finishing a karaoke performance; a new person (Tammy) joins in
jokes.punchline: David has become a full karaoke emcee, introducing 'Tammy' to a crowd with the energy of a hype man — the contrast with his earlier fear of being seen alone is the implicit joke
jokes.explanation: The joke is partly in the callback: David was so afraid of being seen alone, and now he's the life of a bar he arrived at knowing no one. His hype-man energy for 'Tammy!' is also just funny on its face — he's committed entirely to the bit.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Emir asked me if I wanted to stay the night.' David: [audible 'Ohhhh!']
jokes.setup: Stevie comes to find David with news
jokes.punchline: David's enthusiastic reaction ('Ohhhh!') to the sleepover news is both genuinely supportive and theatrically outsized — the warmth is real but his expression of it is always a performance
jokes.explanation: A warm reaction beat. David's outsized 'Ohhhh!' for Stevie's modest romantic update is in character and genuinely sweet. Functional but not a crafted joke per se.

jokes.text: Oh no, I was, but then Tammy kept buying shots, and the whole room got behind me in like, a really big way.
jokes.setup: Stevie expected David would be in bed; he's still at the karaoke bar
jokes.punchline: David has acquired both a benefactor (Tammy and her shots) and a fanbase (the whole room) — his karaoke night has escalated into a small cultural moment
jokes.explanation: A satisfying escalation payoff on David's karaoke arc. 'The whole room got behind me in like, a really big way' is delivered with modest pride that's very funny — he's not bragging, he's slightly surprised by his own success. Warm and character-specific.

jokes.text: Listen, I just wanted you to know that Stevie told me what you did for her tonight. You know, giving up your evening, so that we could have some time together. Anyway, it was really cool of you... and not just because we get to have a little sleepover because of it.
jokes.setup: Emir thanks David for sacrificing his evening for Stevie's sake
jokes.punchline: He ends the sincere thank-you with 'and not just because we get to have a little sleepover because of it' — acknowledging his own self-interest while trying to be gracious, which undercuts the whole speech
jokes.explanation: The self-undermining thank-you is a good structure: Emir builds a sincere speech then admits his ulterior motivation at the end. The 'not just because' construction is the key — he's trying to be generous but can't suppress the honesty. Works nicely.

jokes.text: Oh my God! Hello! Um, hi! Can I get another drink then, to the room, please? Thanks! / I actually just meant like, your mini-bar. / Oh no, I finished that.
jokes.setup: Emir says he 'took care of David's room charges' as a thank-you
jokes.punchline: David immediately orders drinks to the room, assuming full bill coverage; Emir clarifies he meant just the mini-bar; David reveals he's already finished the mini-bar
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed three-beat escalation: David's excitement, Emir's correction, David's reveal that the mini-bar is already gone. The timing of the corrections is sharp and each beat lands. 'Oh no, I finished that' is delivered with just enough breeziness to be very funny.

jokes.text: Hey, Tam! You want some zhampagne?! Yeah, two please, drinks.
jokes.setup: David's mini-bar has been consumed; he's now ordering from the bar with Tammy
jokes.punchline: David calls champagne 'zhampagne' — a vaguely Europeanized affectation — and then the redundant 'yeah, two please, drinks' suggests he's had a great deal of whatever preceded the zhampagne
jokes.explanation: 'Zhampagne' is a fun David-ism — his affected European pronunciation of a fancy word is very in character. The redundant 'yeah, two please, drinks' implies a certain level of intoxication. Less structured than his best lines but a good character detail.

jokes.text: Okay, um... what do you say we get up there, maybe do a little duet? I feel like that's the kind of thing you'd be totally comfortable with, right? / Mhmm... / Yeah, love that. / I think it's best if you guys just sat down, and watched. / Oh, okay. / Next time. / Yeah. Mhmm. / Off the hook for now.
jokes.setup: Emir suggests he and Stevie join David for a karaoke duet
jokes.punchline: David refuses — politely but definitively — relegating them to the audience; 'off the hook for now' implies this is an ongoing negotiation David controls
jokes.explanation: David as karaoke sovereign — rejecting requests from the lovers whose romantic evening he enabled — is a perfect character beat. 'Off the hook for now' treats Emir and Stevie as participants in David's show who've narrowly escaped a performance requirement. Excellent character integration.

jokes.text: I would like to dedicate this one to the lovers right over there! / Okay... / And, goodnight. / David: The la-la-lovers... Cue it up!
jokes.setup: David is about to close the show with a solo
jokes.punchline: He dedicates it to 'the lovers' (Stevie and Emir), making their romance part of his performance; 'And, goodnight' treats his dedication as a complete set-closing bit; 'la-la-lovers' and 'Cue it up!' maintain his headliner persona
jokes.explanation: The payoff of David's entire karaoke arc. He's transformed from a man afraid of being seen alone to a karaoke headliner graciously dedicating songs to his friends' love story. 'La-la-lovers' is a gorgeous stuttering affectation, and 'Cue it up!' with a presumed snap/point finishes the character arc on a perfect comedic note. Earns an 8 for being the satisfying conclusion of a well-built running bit.

jokes.text: Ideally, sometime this year.
jokes.setup: Moira closes the dress scene with optimism about the premiere
jokes.punchline: She repeats 'ideally, sometime this year' — the vague non-commitment from the episode's opening — now as a hopeful touchstone rather than an embarrassing admission
jokes.explanation: A beautiful callback that earns full marks for structure. The phrase that was comic evidence of the premiere's vagueness in act one returns in the closing scene as Moira's sincere battle cry — she's claimed the uncertainty as her own. The repetition transforms a punchline into something almost touching, which is the best kind of Schitt's Creek comedy. Very rewatchable.

jokes.text: And those are the shoes we're going with? / What do you think? / No. / Shoes, later. As well as jewels. / What?!
jokes.setup: The family has a beautiful moment around the dress; Alexis spots the shoes
jokes.punchline: Alexis clocks that the shoes are wrong; Moira agrees ('No') with one devastating word; and then adds that jewels also need to be sorted — revealing that 'sending the dress back' may be more complicated than presented
jokes.explanation: A great closing tag: Moira's 'No' to the shoes is funny in its bluntness, but 'shoes, later. As well as jewels.' reframes the 'send it back' conclusion — she's actually planning an outfit. Johnny's 'What?!' is the perfect audience surrogate reaction. The episode ends on the comedic implication that nothing has been resolved.

tmdb_overview: Johnny finds himself under financial pressure with the arrival of Moira’s high-priced red carpet gown for the Crows movie premiere. David is conned into taking a road trip with Stevie.
insight: Character comedy drives a 76-point episode on modest density and understated emotional payoffs.
